verses.text: And it came to pass in those days, that the Philistines gathered their armies together for warfare, to fight with Israel. And Achish said unto David, Know thou assuredly, that thou shalt go out with me to battle, thou and thy men. And David said to Achish, Surely thou shalt know what thy servant can do. And Achish said to David, Therefore will I make thee keeper of mine head for ever. Guidance: The trap David built for himself in chapter 27 is sprung in two verses. The Philistines muster for major war against Israel. Achish, who has been treating David as his most useful Israelite vassal for sixteen months, calls in the IOU: 'thou shalt go out with me to battle.' Not an invitation — a summons. David's reply is deliciously ambiguous: 'Surely thou shalt know what thy servant can do.' The sentence can be read at least two ways — 'you will see how well I fight' (Achish's reading), or 'you will eventually find out what I am actually capable of in this situation' (the reader's reading). David is buying time with a sentence whose meaning depends on the listener's assumption. Achish, completely persuaded by his own theory of David, reads the loyalty interpretation and rewards it: 'I will make thee keeper of mine head for ever' — David will become Achish's personal bodyguard, the highest honor a vassal could receive. The chapter has put David in the most impossible bind of his life: he is now contracted to fight as a Philistine bodyguard against his own nation. Chapter 29 will reveal how God extricates him. For now, the trap is set.

Now Samuel was dead, and all Israel had lamented him, and buried him in Ramah, even in his own city. And Saul had put away those that had familiar spirits, and the wizards, out of the land. Guidance: Two facts the narrator wants us to hold as we read what follows. (1) Samuel is dead and properly mourned. He was Saul's only legitimate channel of prophetic guidance, and the channel is closed. (2) Saul had previously expelled mediums and necromancers from Israel — in obedience to Deut 18:10-11 and Lev 19:31, 20:6. This is the one official act of religious reform we are told Saul completed; the irony of the chapter is going to be that the man who enforced this law is about to violate it himself. The two notes set up the chapter's structure. The man who needs guidance has no prophet; the man who banned mediums is about to consult one. There is a pattern in scripture of leaders who can be theologically strict about other people's practices while preserving an exemption for themselves at the moment of their own crisis. The chapter is going to expose this exemption mercilessly.

And the Philistines gathered themselves together, and came and pitched in Shunem: and Saul gathered all Israel together, and they pitched in Gilboa. And when Saul saw the host of the Philistines, he was afraid, and his heart greatly trembled. Guidance: The geography of the chapter is fixed. Shunem (Philistine camp) and Gilboa (Israelite camp) are on opposite sides of the Jezreel valley in the north — far from Gibeah, far from Judah, in the wide plain where Philistine chariots can deploy properly. Saul has been forced to fight on terrain that favors his enemy. 'When Saul saw the host of the Philistines, he was afraid, and his heart greatly trembled.' The same king who once mustered three thousand picked men to chase David is now in the field against an army that visibly outnumbers him. Hebrew herod is the verb for the trembling of fear; the narrator is showing us a king physically shaking at the sight of the enemy. The contrast with Jonathan's 'there is no restraint to the LORD to save by many or by few' (14:6) is total. Saul has no theology left to steady him; he has only the numbers, and the numbers are against him.

The same king who once mustered three thousand picked men to chase David is now in the field against an army that visibly outnumbers him. Hebrew herod is the verb for the trembling of fear; the narrator is showing us a king physically shaking at the sight of the enemy. The contrast with Jonathan's 'there is no restraint to the LORD to save by many or by few' (14:6) is total. Saul has no theology left to steady him; he has only the numbers, and the numbers are against him.

And when Saul enquired of the LORD, the LORD answered him not, neither by dreams, nor by Urim, nor by prophets. Guidance: One of the most devastating verses in 1 Samuel. Saul, in his fear, tries to inquire of the LORD. He tries every legitimate channel — three of them, listed in increasing formality. (1) Dreams: the LORD might give him a revelatory dream that night. He does not. (2) Urim: the priestly oracle in the ephod gives yes/no answers; but the ephod is with David (Abiathar in David's camp, 23:6). Saul has no ephod-bearer; the one he could have had is gone because Saul killed the priests at Nob. (3) Prophets: but Samuel is dead, and Saul has no other prophet at his court. The three channels Saul attempts are precisely the three the LORD has blocked. Each silence is the consequence of a previous choice — the spiritual deafness of years, the priests slaughtered, the prophet alienated and dead. The chapter is showing that the silence Saul experiences is not arbitrary divine cruelty; it is the harvest of a long sowing. Sometimes when we cannot hear God, it is because we have spent years progressively removing every way we left for Him to reach us. The verse is the spiritual diagnosis of a man whose self-built isolation is now total.

Then said Saul unto his servants, Seek me a woman that hath a familiar spirit, that I may go to her, and enquire of her. And his servants said to him, Behold, there is a woman that hath a familiar spirit at En-dor. Guidance: Receiving silence from heaven, Saul turns to forbidden practice. 'Seek me a woman that hath a familiar spirit' — Hebrew ba'alat-ov, a woman who masters a 'pit-spirit,' a necromantic medium. The very practice Saul himself banned (v3) is now what he seeks. The servants know where to find one — at En-dor, just north of the Philistine camp, requiring a dangerous detour. Note the disturbing implication: Saul's own servants know the locations of underground mediums even though the practice is officially banned. The ban had driven the practice underground but not out. Saul's enforcement had a public face and a private exception, and now the king himself activates the exception. Sin laws that we enforce on others while permitting ourselves the loophole come back to haunt us at the moment we most need integrity. The man who could not get a word from the LORD will now spend the night listening to a ghost.

And Saul disguised himself, and put on other raiment, and he went, and two men with him, and they came to the woman by night: and he said, I pray thee, divine unto me by the familiar spirit, and bring me him up, whom I shall name unto thee. Guidance: Saul changes out of his royal robes, dons civilian clothes, and goes to En-dor at night with two attendants. The journey requires crossing or skirting Philistine-held territory — En-dor is about four miles northeast of Shunem, where the Philistines were encamped. The disguise is necessary because the medium would recognize Saul, and (per v9) would fear punishment. The night-travel and disguise are all the more pathetic given that Saul is supposed to be leading his army in the morning. The king of Israel is sneaking through enemy lines to get a séance the night before battle. 'Divine unto me by the familiar spirit, and bring me him up, whom I shall name unto thee.' Saul is not vague about the practice; he names it correctly. He knows exactly what he is asking for. There is no plausible deniability; this is conscious participation in a practice he himself outlawed.

And the woman said unto him, Behold, thou knowest what Saul hath done, how he hath cut off those that have familiar spirits, and the wizards, out of the land: wherefore then layest thou a snare for my life, to cause me to die? And Saul sware to her by the LORD, saying, As the LORD liveth, there shall no punishment happen to thee for this thing. Guidance: The medium is cautious. She does not recognize the disguised Saul, but she suspects entrapment: 'thou knowest what Saul hath done, how he hath cut off those that have familiar spirits.' The verse confirms that Saul's ban on mediums was real and enforced; she has lived in fear of being caught. The detail is darkly ironic — the very king she fears is standing in front of her asking her to commit the offense she would die for. Saul's response is the chapter's most blasphemous moment: 'As the LORD liveth, there shall no punishment happen to thee for this thing.' Saul swears in the LORD's name to protect a person doing what the LORD has explicitly forbidden. He invokes the God whose silence drove him here as the guarantor of his violation of God's law. The combination of formal piety and active disobedience is the moral nadir of Saul's reign. He cannot get a word from God, so he swears by God's name to protect a witch from a punishment God commanded. The theological self-contradiction is total.

And the woman said unto him, Behold, thou knowest what Saul hath done, how he hath cut off those that have familiar spirits, and the wizards, out of the land: wherefore then layest thou a snare for my life, to cause me to die? And Saul sware to her by the LORD, saying, As the LORD liveth, there shall no punishment happen to thee for this thing. Guidance: The medium is cautious. The verse confirms that Saul's ban on mediums was real and enforced; she has lived in fear of being caught. The detail is darkly ironic — the very king she fears is standing in front of her asking her to commit the offense she would die for. Saul's response is the chapter's most blasphemous moment: 'As the LORD liveth, there shall no punishment happen to thee for this thing.' Saul swears in the LORD's name to protect a person doing what the LORD has explicitly forbidden. He invokes the God whose silence drove him here as the guarantor of his violation of God's law. The combination of formal piety and active disobedience is the moral nadir of Saul's reign. He cannot get a word from God, so he swears by God's name to protect a witch from a punishment God commanded. The theological self-contradiction is total.

Then said the woman, Whom shall I bring up unto thee? And he said, Bring me up Samuel. Guidance: 'Bring me up Samuel.' The man Saul has spent fifteen years trying to avoid hearing from is now the one man he wants to hear from. The man whose alive-and-speaking voice Saul refused to obey he now wants to summon from the dead. The chapter is one of the bitterest portraits in scripture of how much harder it is to hear a word from God after years of refusing the word you had. The same Samuel who in chapter 15 had told Saul plainly that the LORD had rejected him is the only voice Saul can think of who might still help. Saul does not want Samuel to forgive him; he wants Samuel to instruct him on tactical military strategy. He still thinks the problem is information about tomorrow's battle, not the long disobedience that has brought him to this night. The diagnosis Samuel will give is not the diagnosis Saul wants; Samuel is going to tell him not what to do but what will happen — Saul will die tomorrow with his sons. Sometimes God's mercy is to refuse to give us the tactical answer because the tactical question was never the real question.

And when the woman saw Samuel, she cried with a loud voice: and the woman spake to Saul, saying, Why hast thou deceived me? for thou art Saul. And the king said unto her, Be not afraid: for what sawest thou? And the woman said unto Saul, I saw gods ascending out of the earth. And he said unto her, What form is he of? And she said, An old man cometh up; and he is covered with a mantle. And Saul perceived that it was Samuel, and he stooped with his face to the ground, and bowed himself. Guidance: The séance unfolds. The woman screams when Samuel appears — apparently this is not what usually happens; her standard practice was probably some kind of theatrical performance with no actual spirit appearing. This time something real comes up, and her own scream betrays her surprise. Theologically, scholars have debated whether God Himself supernaturally sends Samuel as a one-time exception to expose Saul, or whether the woman conjures an ordinary demonic apparition; the text simply says she sees Samuel and Samuel speaks. The most natural reading is that God, by extraordinary providence, permits a real appearance of Samuel as a final mercy of judgment — using the medium's session as the vehicle, even though the practice itself is condemned. (The Hebrew word 'elohim' the woman uses — 'gods ascending' — can mean simply 'a divine being' or 'a ghostly figure'; she is describing what she sees.) Her shock at the appearance is what unmasks Saul: 'thou art Saul.' Only the king of Israel would have warranted a real appearance of Samuel. Saul confirms his identity, and asks for the description. The figure is 'an old man covered with a mantle' — the very mantle Saul tore in chapter 15 (15:27). The visual cue identifies him precisely. Saul bows to the ground; the king who refused to bow to Samuel's living word now prostrates before his ghost.

And Samuel said to Saul, Why hast thou disquieted me, to bring me up? And Saul answered, I am sore distressed; for the Philistines make war against me, and God is departed from me, and answereth me no more, neither by prophets, nor by dreams: therefore I have called thee, that thou mayest make known unto me what I shall do. Guidance: Samuel's first words are exasperated: 'Why hast thou disquieted me, to bring me up?' The verb 'disquieted' (ragaz) implies disturbing someone's rest; Samuel is in some kind of restful state from which Saul's summons has dragged him. The detail tells us the Old Testament had some intuition of the righteous dead being at rest — a hint of what later resurrection theology will develop. Saul's answer is honest desperation, naming the situation precisely: 'I am sore distressed; for the Philistines make war against me, and God is departed from me, and answereth me no more, neither by prophets, nor by dreams.' He names three things: the Philistine threat, the LORD's departure, the silence of all legitimate channels. (He omits Urim, the third channel from v6 — perhaps because mentioning the priestly oracle would expose his own role in the Nob massacre.) 'Therefore I have called thee, that thou mayest make known unto me what I shall do.' The request is still tactical — what do I do tomorrow? Saul has not yet asked what he should do about his life as a whole; he is still asking only about the battle. He will not get the answer he wants.

Then said Samuel, Wherefore then dost thou ask of me, seeing the LORD is departed from thee, and is become thine enemy? And the LORD hath done to him, as he spake by me: for the LORD hath rent the kingdom out of thine hand, and given it to thy neighbour, even to David. Guidance: Samuel's reply is mercilessly direct. 'Wherefore then dost thou ask of me, seeing the LORD is departed from thee, and is become thine enemy?' The grammar is brutal: God is 'become thine enemy' (ar — adversary). Asking a dead prophet was never going to extract guidance from a God who has positioned Himself in opposition. The prophet cannot dispense oracles when the heart of the matter is the speaker's enmity with God. Then Samuel restates the chapter-15 verdict: 'The LORD hath rent the kingdom out of thine hand, and given it to thy neighbour, even to David.' For the first time, Samuel uses David's name in the verdict. Back in 15:28, Samuel had said only 'a neighbour better than thou'; the identification has now been made explicit by all of subsequent history. The man whose name Saul has not been able to say without rage is now spoken by the prophet's ghost as the appointed heir. Saul cannot escape what he refused to accept. There comes a point in a life of resistance to God's word where the word becomes a wall that no amount of pleading and séance and travel can get around.

Because thou obeyedst not the voice of the LORD, nor executedst his fierce wrath upon Amalek, therefore hath the LORD done this thing unto thee this day. Moreover the LORD will also deliver Israel with thee into the hand of the Philistines: and tomorrow shalt thou and thy sons be with me: the LORD also shall deliver the host of Israel into the hand of the Philistines. Guidance: Samuel names the original sin: Amalek. The Saul story has now traced its disaster back to its precise origin — chapter 15. The selective obedience that spared Agag and the best livestock has produced, through twenty years of compounding moral decay, the present scene at Endor. Then comes the prophecy of doom: 'tomorrow shalt thou and thy sons be with me.' One sentence, four catastrophes. (1) Saul will die tomorrow. (2) His sons will die — including Jonathan. (3) The army of Israel will be delivered to the Philistines. (4) 'With me' — Saul will be where Samuel is, in the realm of the dead. The prophecy is precise and complete. Tomorrow, on Gilboa, the king and his three sons will all fall. The dynasty will end. Israel will lose the battle. The reign of Saul ends at sundown on the day after this séance. The chapter is showing us a man receiving the precise schedule of his own death. The mercy in it — and there is one — is that Saul has one final night to make peace with what is coming. Whether he uses it for that or not, the next verses will reveal.

Guidance: Samuel names the original sin: Amalek. The Saul story has now traced its disaster back to its precise origin — chapter 15. Whether he uses it for that or not, the next verses will reveal.

Then Saul fell straightway all along on the earth, and was sore afraid, because of the words of Samuel: and there was no strength in him; for he had eaten no bread all the day, nor all the night. Guidance: Saul collapses. 'Fell straightway all along on the earth' — full prone, face down. The combination of physical and emotional shock drops him to the ground. The narrator adds the medical-realist note: he has not eaten the entire day or the previous night — likely a war-fast he had imposed on himself in the desperate hope that ritual abstention would compel a divine answer (like Saul's previous oath-fast in 14:24, which had brought no military success then either, only suffering). The fast did not produce an oracle; it produced bodily weakness. Saul is now a king physically unable to stand on the night before the battle he must lead. The verse is one of the saddest visual in 1 Samuel — the tall man (1 Samuel 9:2 told us he was head and shoulders above the people) lying full-length on the dirt of a witch's house, unable to rise, the night before he must lead an army to certain defeat. The throne of Israel has been reduced to this. There is a particular degradation in being unable to rise from the floor of the very place you went to in violation of your own law.

And the woman came unto Saul, and saw that he was sore troubled, and said unto him, Behold, thine handmaid hath obeyed thy voice, and I have put my life in my hand, and have hearkened unto thy words which thou spakest unto me. Now therefore, I pray thee, hearken thou also unto the voice of thine handmaid, and let me set a morsel of bread before thee; and eat, that thou mayest have strength, when thou goest on thy way. Guidance: The medium — the very person Saul came to use, the woman whose practice he had banned — becomes the most compassionate figure in the chapter. She comes to the prostrate king and offers, in essence, hospitality. 'I have put my life in my hand and have hearkened unto thy words.' She names the risk she has taken and the obedience she has shown him. 'Now hearken thou also unto the voice of thine handmaid.' She asks for one favor: let me feed you. 'Eat, that thou mayest have strength, when thou goest on thy way.' She knows he is going to his death; she does not say so, but she knows. The chapter is showing us a woman whose profession is forbidden but whose conduct toward a broken man is more merciful than the conduct of any of his senior officials. The contrast with Doeg (the man who carried out the Nob massacre with relish) is striking — the Edomite herdsman would not stop killing; the En-dor medium will not stop feeding. The boundaries of God's image in His creatures do not always run along the lines of their religious credentials. There are kindnesses available to a king from people he had outlawed that he could not have received from people he had honored.

And the woman came unto Saul, and saw that he was sore troubled, and said unto him, Behold, thine handmaid hath obeyed thy voice, and I have put my life in my hand, and have hearkened unto thy words which thou spakest unto me. Now therefore, I pray thee, hearken thou also unto the voice of thine handmaid, and let me set a morsel of bread before thee; and eat, that thou mayest have strength, when thou goest on thy way. 'Eat, that thou mayest have strength, when thou goest on thy way.' She knows he is going to his death; she does not say so, but she knows. The chapter is showing us a woman whose profession is forbidden but whose conduct toward a broken man is more merciful than the conduct of any of his senior officials. The contrast with Doeg (the man who carried out the Nob massacre with relish) is striking — the Edomite herdsman would not stop killing; the En-dor medium will not stop feeding. The boundaries of God's image in His creatures do not always run along the lines of their religious credentials. There are kindnesses available to a king from people he had outlawed that he could not have received from people he had honored.

But he refused, and said, I will not eat. But his servants, together with the woman, compelled him; and he hearkened unto their voice. So he arose from the earth, and sat upon the bed. And the woman had a fat calf in the house; and she hasted, and killed it, and took flour, and kneaded it, and did bake unleavened bread thereof: And she brought it before Saul, and before his servants; and they did eat. Then they rose up, and went away that night. Guidance: Saul refuses at first; his servants and the woman together press him until he agrees. He rises from the floor and sits on the bed. The woman moves quickly — kills a fat calf (a substantial gift of meat, the kind reserved for honoring an important guest), kneads flour, bakes unleavened bread (probably because there is no time to wait for leavening). The meal she serves is, by any measure, the meal one would serve a king. The detail is heart-piercing: the medium of Endor honors the king Israel cannot honor anymore. She gives Saul the kind of meal his own court has not provided that day. He eats with his servants and they leave at night, back through Philistine-held territory, to the Israelite encampment on Gilboa where dawn will bring the battle in which Saul will die. The chapter ends in the dark. The next time we see Saul will be in chapter 31, falling on his own sword on the ridge of Gilboa, with his three sons dead beside him and the Philistine army closing in. The tragedy of his reign is complete; what remains is only the form of its ending. The chapter has shown us the spiritual cause; chapter 31 will show us the historical effect. Between them is one night and one battle. We part with Saul knowing what he knows — that tomorrow he will die, and that the kingdom is David's, and that the long resistance has ended at last in the dust of a forbidden room. Even there, even at the end, God has arranged for a meal. Not because Saul deserves it; not because the woman is righteous; but because the LORD is the kind of God who lets His enemies eat one more time before the verdict lands. The chapter is a final dignity, even toward a king under judgment. It is the last meal anyone serves Saul. He goes from her table to the battlefield of his death.

But he refused, and said, I will not eat. But his servants, together with the woman, compelled him; and he hearkened unto their voice. So he arose from the earth, and sat upon the bed. And the woman had a fat calf in the house; and she hasted, and killed it, and took flour, and kneaded it, and did bake unleavened bread thereof: And she brought it before Saul, and before his servants; and they did eat. Then they rose up, and went away that night. Guidance: Saul refuses at first; his servants and the woman together press him until he agrees. He rises from the floor and sits on the bed. The woman moves quickly — kills a fat calf (a substantial gift of meat, the kind reserved for honoring an important guest), kneads flour, bakes unleavened bread (probably because there is no time to wait for leavening). The meal she serves is, by any measure, the meal one would serve a king. The detail is heart-piercing: the medium of Endor honors the king Israel cannot honor anymore. She gives Saul the kind of meal his own court has not provided that day. He eats with his servants and they leave at night, back through Philistine-held territory, to the Israelite encampment on Gilboa where dawn will bring the battle in which Saul will die. The chapter ends in the dark. The next time we see Saul will be in chapter 31, falling on his own sword on the ridge of Gilboa, with his three sons dead beside him and the Philistine army closing in. The tragedy of his reign is complete; what remains is only the form of its ending. The chapter has shown us the spiritual cause; chapter 31 will show us the historical effect. Between them is one night and one battle. We part with Saul knowing what he knows — that tomorrow he will die, and that the kingdom is David's, and that the long resistance has ended at last in the dust of a forbidden room. Even there, even at the end, God has arranged for a meal. Not because Saul deserves it; not because the woman is righteous; but because the LORD is the kind of God who lets His enemies eat one more time before the verdict lands. The chapter is a final dignity, even toward a king under judgment. It is the last meal anyone serves Saul. He goes from her table to the battlefield of his death.
